title: Fix Your Heart or Die: Adrian Chiarella on Leviticus
description: Leviticus (2026), Adrian Chiarella’s debut feature, begins with an archetypal horror image: a “little death” that begets a
hook: A filmmaker turns a brutal true crime into intimate family mythology—how trauma becomes art when you're brave enough to fictionalize it.

title: Robby Hoffman Thinks There Are Worse Things Than Being Offended
description: The standup comic and “Hacks” actor thinks the conversation about class in America is long overdue, and she’ll keep talking about it until people listen.
hook: A "Hacks" actor argues class anxiety is America's real taboo—funnier and meaner than the outrage conversations we're actually having.

title: Dad Died and All I Got Was This Angry Alpaca
description:
A Report on the Alpaca One day the alpaca kicked and bit the man from Milwaukee who had moved to Tulum to open and operate an animal sanctuary. As the man was dying, the alpaca stopped biting his face, crouched, and sat on him. The man from Milwaukee’s neighbor Ted came over. “I’m sorry this […]

The post Dad Died and All I Got Was This Angry Alpaca appeared first on
hook: A man moves to Tulum to run an alpaca sanctuary. The alpaca kills him anyway. This darkly funny report sits at the exact intersection of absurdity and grief.

title: Dooneen by Keith Ridgway review – uncanny visions of dark times in Dublin
description:
Ireland is trembling with nascent social unrest in this labyrinthine tale of one man’s homecoming

Irish author Keith Ridgway’s latest novel deals, both mischievously and menacingly, in ambivalence. The book’s epigraph is taken from a misty-eyed ballad pining for the “lofty” magnificence of the Cliffs of Dooneen. But these lines are appended with a footnote cautioning that “debate continues concerning the cliffs named in the song – whether they are in County Clare or County Kerry, o
hook: Dublin trembles with social unrest in Ridgway's labyrinthine new novel—one homecoming told through uncanny, menacing ambivalence.

title: Don’t Ask Lari Pittman What His Work Is “About”
description: The Colombian-American painter discusses his new body of work, the complexity of queerness, and the reprieve of the analog.
hook: A Colombian-American painter refuses to explain his work's "meaning"—instead he talks queerness, complexity, and why analog still matters.
